hints: The origin is between the two points iff one is negative and the other positive.
solution: The origin lies between the two points iff one point is negative and the other positive. On $[-1,2]$ (length $3$), a point is negative with probability $\tfrac13$ and positive with probability $\tfrac23$. So $P=2\cdot\tfrac13\cdot\tfrac23=\tfrac49.$

hints: The visit order is a permutation of 1..8 starting at 1; a leftward hop is a descent. Want exactly one descent.
A one-descent permutation starting at 1 = assign each of {2..8} to a first/second increasing run; each non-identity assignment gives exactly one descent.
solution: The frog's visit order is a permutation of $\{1,\dots,8\}$ starting at $1$ ($7!=5040$ equally likely). A leftward hop is a descent, and we want exactly one. A permutation with one descent splits into two increasing runs; assign each of $\{2,\dots,8\}$ to run 1 or run 2 ($2^7=128$ ways). Every assignment except the $8$ that reproduce the fully-increasing identity gives exactly one descent, and distinct one-descent permutations come from distinct assignments. So there are $128-8=120$ favorable orders, giving $\dfrac{120}{5040}=\dfrac{1}{42}.$

hints: This is the Monty Hall problem.
Your first pick is right w.p. 1/3; the host's reveal gives no reason to change that.
Switching wins exactly when your first pick was wrong: 2/3.
solution: Classic Monty Hall. Your initial door hides the car with probability $\tfrac13$, and the host's informed reveal doesn't change that. Switching wins precisely when your first choice was a goat, which happens with probability $\tfrac23.$
With $A=\pi r^2$, $\dfrac{dA}{dt}=2\pi r\dfrac{dr}{dt}$. After $10$ s, $r=20$ ft and $\dfrac{dr}{dt}=2$, so $\dfrac{dA}{dt}=2\pi(20)(2)=80\pi$, giving $k=80.$

hints: A bounce is indistinguishable from the two ants passing through each other.
So the trajectories just pass through: every left-moving ant exits Alice's side, every right-moving exits Bob's.
Reach Alice = Bob's 80, reach Bob = Alice's 40 ⇒ x−y = 80−40 = 40.
solution: Treat each collision as the two ants passing through each other (swap identities) — the set of trajectories is unchanged. Then every ant headed toward Alice exits at Alice's end and vice versa. Bob's $80$ ants head toward Alice and Alice's $40$ head toward Bob, so $x=80$ reach Alice and $y=40$ reach Bob, giving $x-y=40.$

hints: Use ratios of consecutive binomials: C(n,k−1)/C(n,k)=k/(n−k+1).
solution: For consecutive entries $\binom{n}{k-1},\binom{n}{k},\binom{n}{k+1}$, the ratios are $\dfrac{k}{n-k+1}=\dfrac34$ and $\dfrac{k+1}{n-k}=\dfrac45$. The first gives $7k=3n+3$, the second $9k=4n-5$. Eliminating $k$: $\dfrac{3n+3}{7}=\dfrac{4n-5}{9}\Rightarrow 27n+27=28n-35\Rightarrow n=62.$

hints: For a random function each element is a fixed point independently w.p. 1/n.
solution: For a uniformly random function $f:S_n\to S_n$, each $i$ is a fixed point independently with probability $\tfrac1n$, so $F_n\sim\mathrm{Binom}(n,\tfrac1n)$, which converges to $\mathrm{Poisson}(1)$. Hence $\lim_n P(F_n=5)=\dfrac{e^{-1}1^5}{5!}=\dfrac{1}{120e}$, so $c=\dfrac1{120}.$

hints: Primes on a die are {2,3,5}, so success probability is 1/2.
solution: The primes on a die are $2,3,5$, so each roll succeeds with probability $\tfrac36=\tfrac12$, and the count is $\mathrm{Binom}(6,\tfrac12)$. Thus $P(\text{exactly }3)=\binom{6}{3}\big(\tfrac12\big)^6=\dfrac{20}{64}=\dfrac{5}{16}.$

hints: Fix the youngest person; the other 6 can be seated in 6! ways.
Only 2 of these are increasing around the circle (clockwise or counterclockwise).
solution: Anchor the youngest person to break circular symmetry; the remaining $6$ occupy the other seats in $6!=720$ ways. Increasing order around the table happens in exactly $2$ of these (clockwise or counterclockwise). So $P=\dfrac{2}{720}=\dfrac1{360}.$

hints: Each draw is blue w.p. 1/6, green w.p. 1/6, other w.p. 4/6 (with replacement).
Use complement: 1 − P(no blue) − P(no green) + P(neither).
solution:
With replacement, each draw is blue w.p. $\tfrac16$, green w.p. $\tfrac16$. By inclusion–exclusion on the complements,
$$P(\ge1\text{ blue and }\ge1\text{ green})=1-2\Big(\tfrac56\Big)^4+\Big(\tfrac46\Big)^4=1-\tfrac{1250}{1296}+\tfrac{256}{1296}=\tfrac{302}{1296}=\tfrac{151}{648}.$$

hints: C=(5/9)(F−32): the mean shifts and scales, but variance only scales by (5/9)².
solution: Under $C=\tfrac59(F-32)$, the mean transforms fully: $\mu_C=\tfrac59(80-32)=\tfrac{240}{9}\approx26.667$. Variance ignores the shift and scales by $(\tfrac59)^2$: $\sigma_C^2=\tfrac{25}{81}\cdot25=\tfrac{625}{81}\approx7.716$. The absolute difference is $|26.667-7.716|\approx18.95.$

Pooled variance: $s_p^2=\dfrac{49(9^2)+49(10^2)}{98}=90.5$. Standard error $=\sqrt{90.5(\tfrac1{50}+\tfrac1{50})}\approx1.903$, so $t=\dfrac{74-71}{1.903}\approx1.577$ on $98$ df. The two-tailed attained significance is $2\,P(T_{98}>1.577)\approx 0.1142.$
